commands: WebSearch: dissipative bulk flow GW template Lewicki 2025 dbf validity range supercooling calibration
WebSearch: Coleman-Weinberg conformal Abelian Higgs renormalization scale uncertainty RG running g^4 approximation validity
WebSearch: PTArcade ceffyl convergence criteria GW spectrum likelihood N_kernel sampling efficiency
WebSearch: mean bubble separation R* versus beta/H supercooled phase transition strong parameter breakdown
WebSearch: supercooled phase transition reheating temperature T_reh calculation alpha factor GW spectrum peak frequency shift nHz
WebSearch: dark photon invisible decay A' -> A'* rho three-body final state branching fraction strong coupling
warnings: The Delta N_eff constraint (xi=1 leads to Delta N_eff~0.57, exceeding Planck bound of 0.3) is the most serious uncontrolled uncertainty affecting the model's cosmological viability
The portal couplings epsilon and lambda_HPhi are completely unspecified, making 19 out of 23 constraints untestable without further assumptions
The beta/H parameterization may be invalid for the extreme supercooling regime (alpha~10^11-10^15) where mean bubble separation R* is the proper variable
The PTArcade inference used partial chains (~5700 post-burn-in samples from 1e5 total), and formal convergence diagnostics (R-hat, effective sample size) were not reported
Exact reheating temperature and its effect on the GW peak frequency were not computed self-consistently within the pipeline
No 3D EFT or RG-improved effective potential was used; the fixed-scale CW approximation with log terms at chi=chi0 is known from arXiv:2511.02910 to differ from 2-loop 3D EFT results
approximations: High-temperature expansion (small phi/T) used for effective potential
Gaussian approximation for false vacuum fraction
Backend renormalization scale set to vev (no explicit RG running)
f_peak_est uses fixed g* = 10 approximation for MeV-scale
GW spectrum uses dbf template from Lewicki et al. 2025 (arXiv:2511.15687)
No non-Gaussianity, polarisation, or frequency-dependent speed of sound effects modeled
Temperature ratio xi = T_dark/T_SM = 1 (thermal equilibrium) hardcoded
Dark sector contains only gauge boson A' and radial mode rho; no fermionic content
PTArcade log-likelihood uses CEFFyL method for NG15 dataset
Runaway bubble walls (vw -> 1) assumed for all viable points
Standard beta/H defined through S_3/T expansion, assumes exponential nucleation regime

upstream_statuses.notes: Validated model specification with 5 warnings about unspecified portals, xi=1 assumption, RG scheme dependence, no DM candidate
0 red flags, 5 warnings. Model passed all validation checks.
Literature search: 10 papers reviewed. Model declared not_novel. Literature benchmarks provided.
925 scan points, 680 viable. 204 numerical failures, 41 physical failures (eternal inflation). 243 PTA-band points.
Benchmark scan (1600 points, 524 viable) + PTArcade campaign (1e5 samples, ~30k post-thinning). Posterior: v~0.62-1.23 GeV, g_D~0.509-0.545 (1-sigma).
23 constraints considered. 4 directly testable (all passed), 6 approximately testable, 3 recast-needed, 4 not applicable. 19 of 23 constraints blocked by unspecified portal couplings.

assumptions.description: Classical scale invariance at tree level: no dimensionful parameters in the Lagrangian. Scale breaking purely radiative via CW mechanism.
assumptions.required_solution: Already verified by critic-agent: tree-level Lagrangian has no mass terms. Remains a controlled assumption as long as higher-order operators or Planck-scale suppressed terms do not reintroduce dimensionful parameters.

source.note: Tree-level scale invariance is a defining feature of the model. No mass terms present, consistent with CSI.

assumptions.description: No fermionic dark matter in the dark sector. The only new particles are complex scalar Phi and gauge boson A'.
assumptions.required_solution: This is a deliberate model choice. It simplifies the analysis but means fewer experimental handles. Adding a fermion would change the CW beta function (fermions contribute negatively) and affect the FOPT parameters. The model has no DM candidate, which is itself a feature not a bug.

source.note: No fermions listed in particle content. Critic-agent notes this as 'info' severity warning.

assumptions.description: Portal couplings (kinetic mixing epsilon, Higgs portal lambda_HPhi) are assumed small enough to not affect FOPT dynamics, but their values are completely unspecified.
assumptions.required_solution: Specify numerical upper bounds or benchmark values for epsilon and lambda_HPhi. Compute the resulting thermalization rate, decay widths, and branching fractions. This is the single most impactful missing information in the pipeline.

source.note: epsilon range 'unspecified, assumed small', lambda_HPhi range 'unspecified, assumed small'. Critically, these are needed for both verifying xi=1 and applying experimental constraints.

assumptions.description: xi = T_dark/T_SM = 1: dark and visible sectors are fully thermalized at the same temperature.
affected_outputs: GW spectrum amplitude and peak frequency
assumptions.required_solution: This assumption is hardcoded in the backend (self.xi=1) and cannot be scanned. The literature (Li & Nath 2026, arXiv:2602.14324) shows that treating xi as a dynamic variable can change GW predictions by up to 4 orders of magnitude. Additionally, xi=1 leads to Delta N_eff~0.57 > Planck bound. Compute thermalization conditions and either scan xi or implement synchronous T evolution.

source.note: xi = 1 fixed/hardcoded. Listed as an explicit assumption with critic warning about tension with small portal couplings.

assumptions.description: Portal couplings are simultaneously small enough to not affect FOPT dynamics but large enough to thermalize the dark sector (xi=1). This creates a quantitative tension that is not resolved.
assumptions.required_solution: Quantify the required epsilon for thermalization at MeV-scale temperatures (typically epsilon >= 10^{-10} from the literature). Verify that this value is consistent with 'small enough for FOPT'. Compute the actual thermalization rate and dark-visible equilibration time. Note: Coy et al. 2025 (arXiv:2405.10792) gives BBN lower bound epsilon > 10^{-9}-10^{-8} for m_A'~300-700 MeV, which set a floor.

source.note: Warning item 'xi=1 assumption vs thermalization requirements'

assumptions.description: No tree-level cross-quartic interaction with SM Higgs, only the portal coupling lambda_{HPhi}|H|^2|Phi|^2 which is assumed small. The FOPT potential is purely dark-sector.
assumptions.required_solution: For now, this is consistent with the dark-sector-focused analysis. If lambda_HPhi were large, it would modify the effective potential, change the vacuum structure, and alter FOPT dynamics. Can be checked once lambda_HPhi is quantified.

source.note: Higgs portal listed as optional and assumed small. Tree-level potential has no cross terms.

assumptions.description: One-loop CW approximation with fixed-scale logs: backend evaluates log terms at chi = chi0 (the vev), with no RG improvement or scale variation.
affected_outputs: FOPT parameters (alpha, beta/H, Tn, Tp, Treh)
assumptions.required_solution: Christiansen et al. (2025, arXiv:2511.02910) explicitly studied the conformal Abelian Higgs model and found that proper RG running is essential. Their comparison of 4D high-T with daisy at mu=pi T vs 2-loop 3D EFT shows differences. The fixed-scale approximation at chi=chi0 may produce quantitatively different alpha and beta/H values. A dedicated comparison using the RG-improved potential (e.g., following arXiv:2501.11619 which uses mu = max[M_Z'(phi_s), pi T]) would quantify this uncertainty.

source.note: 'Backend renormalization scale set to vev (no RG running)'. Also flagged by critic-agent as warning.

assumptions.description: No explicit Daisy resummation beyond the Arnold-Espinosa scheme implemented in the backend.
assumptions.required_solution: The Arnold-Espinosa resummation is the standard approach and is widely used. The 3D EFT methods (as in arXiv:2511.02910) are more rigorous but give consistent results when RG scale is mu=pi T. This is considered controlled for current precision levels.

source.note: 'Daisy resummation via Arnold-Espinosa scheme'

assumptions.description: High-temperature expansion (small chi/T expansion) used for thermal effective potential.
affected_outputs: FOPT parameters for the most supercooled points
assumptions.required_solution: The high-T expansion is valid for m/T << 1. However, in the deep supercooling regime (alpha ~ 10^11-10^15), the nucleation and percolation occur at very low T ~ 10^-6 to 10^-4 GeV. In this regime, the high-T expansion is near or beyond its formal validity limit since the dark sector particles are non-relativistic (m_A'/T ~ O(10-1000)). The backend still uses this expansion, which may introduce systematic errors. A low-temperature expansion or full numerical evaluation of the thermal integrals would be more appropriate for the extreme supercooling regime.

source.note: 'High-temperature expansion (small phi/T) used for effective potential'. Also noted in literature (arXiv:2511.02910) that 3D EFT is more reliable for supercooled regime.

assumptions.description: Gaussian approximation for the false vacuum fraction (nucleation rate approximated as Gaussian around peak).
assumptions.required_solution: The Gaussian approximation is standard and works well for most transitions. For extremely supercooled transitions (alpha >> 1), the nucleation rate may deviate from a simple Gaussian shape (see arXiv:1611.05853). The mean bubble separation R* approach advocated in arXiv:2501.15649 and arXiv:2602.14324 provides a more robust alternative. The current beta/H values (17-35) are positive, suggesting the Gaussian approximation is still valid, but this should be verified against a direct bounce action evaluation.

source.note: 'Gaussian approximation for false vacuum fraction'

assumptions.description: beta/H parameterization used to describe the inverse duration of the phase transition. This assumes the exponential nucleation regime holds.
affected_outputs: GW spectrum amplitude and peak frequency
assumptions.required_solution: For ultra-strong supercooling (alpha >> 1), the standard beta/H approximation for the mean bubble separation R* ~ (8pi)^(1/3) v_w / beta breaks down. The literature (arXiv:2003.08892, arXiv:2602.14324, arXiv:2501.15649) shows that beta/H can become small or even negative, and the proper variable is the mean bubble separation R* = n_b^{-1/3} computed from strict integration of the nucleation history. Several PTA-fit papers (e.g., arXiv:2501.15649, arXiv:2602.14324) use R* explicitly. The current pipeline uses beta/H throughout; a dedicated R* computation would provide a cross-check.

source.note: Listed as theory uncertainty: 'PT parameter definitions: the beta/H parameter is conventional for weak transitions but breaks down for strong supercooling'

assumptions.description: GW spectrum uses the dissipative bulk flow (dbf) template from Lewicki et al. 2025 (arXiv:2511.15687), which is the single template choice.
assumptions.required_solution: The dbf template is the most appropriate for the strong-supercooling regime (alpha >> 1) and was selected over the 'higgsless' (sound-wave) and 'bf' (bulk flow) templates. However, using a single template introduces systematic uncertainty in the GW spectrum modeling. The literature (arXiv:2409.03651) shows that different templates can give different spectral shapes. A template-averaging or marginalization approach (similar to what some PTA analyses do for SMBHB models) would capture this uncertainty. The current dbf template assumes runaway walls.

source.note: dbf template chosen. 'higgsless' rejected as invalid for alpha >> 1.

assumptions.description: The dbf template calibration range may be pushed for the most extreme alpha values (10^11-10^15) found in this model.
affected_outputs: GW spectrum amplitude for extreme alpha points
assumptions.required_solution: The dbf template from Lewicki et al. (2025) was calibrated for alpha in the strong-supercooling regime, but the most extreme values here (alpha ~ 10^11-10^15) may exceed the calibration range. The spectral shape (low-f f^2, high-f fall-off) remains physically motivated, but the absolute normalization could have additional uncertainty. The PTA-agent noted this caveat. A dedicated simulation study or comparison with alternative templates at extreme alpha would be needed to quantify this.

source.note: WARNING: 'For the most extreme alpha values (10^11-10^15), the template's prefactor may be pushing its calibration range'

assumptions.description: Runaway bubble walls (v_w -> 1) assumed for all viable points, consistent with the dbf template assumption.
assumptions.required_solution: Supercooled FOPTs with alpha >> 1 are expected to have runaway walls because the vacuum energy dominates. However, NLO friction from soft gauge boson emission (Bodeker-Moore pressure) can limit the terminal wall velocity, as discussed in arXiv:2501.15649. The current pipeline does not compute friction or check whether walls are truly runaway. The GW amplitude scales strongly with v_w, so a non-runaway scenario could suppress the signal significantly.

source.note: Assumption: 'Runaway bubble walls (v_w -> 1) typical of supercooled transitions'

assumptions.description: Exact reheating temperature T_reh and its detailed effect on the GW peak frequency are not computed self-consistently. The f_peak_est uses a fixed g*=10 approximation.
assumptions.required_solution: The backend computes T_reh from energy conservation (rho_vac -> radiation), but the precise value depends on the evolution of g*(T) through the reheating process. The fixed g*=10 approximation for MeV-scale temperatures is reasonable but introduces O(10-30%) uncertainty in T_reh and hence in the redshifted GW peak frequency. Additionally, the reheating dynamics (instantaneous vs gradual) can affect the GW spectrum shape (arXiv:2603.11184), which is not modeled here.

source.note: 'f_peak_est uses fixed g* = 10 approximation for MeV-scale'. Also flagged by critic-agent as part of RG scheme dependence discussion.

assumptions.description: CW-fixed scalar quartic lambda_Phi is not scanned independently; it is determined by beta_lambda = 3*g_D^4/(8*pi^2) at the minimum.
assumptions.required_solution: This is correct by construction for the CW mechanism: the quartic coupling is not a free parameter. The CW minimization condition relates lambda_Phi at the RG scale to g_D. Scanning lambda_Phi independently would break the conformal symmetry breaking mechanism. This is a controlled approximation.

source.note: 'CW-fixed scalar quartic (not scanned independently)'

assumptions.description: The model is not novel (verdict from preliminary-librarian-agent). arXiv:2501.11619 studied the same class of model using RG-improved potentials and CosmoTransitions, providing direct benchmark comparison.
assumptions.required_solution: The non-novelty verdict is well-documented with direct literature citations. The specific analysis (minimal model without DM, using the semianalytic_pipeline backend with dbf template) provides a complementary perspective but does not claim new physics discovery. This is controlled as long as the report agent acknowledges the existing literature.

source.note: Verdict: 'not_novel'. Multiple papers study the same model class.

assumptions.description: PTArcade Bayesian inference convergence: N_samples=1e5, ~5700 post-thinning post-burn-in samples. Formal convergence diagnostics (R-hat, effective sample size) not reported.
assumptions.required_solution: The PTArcade campaign used 1e5 total samples. After thinning and burn-in, approximately 5700 samples remain. While this may be sufficient for 2-dimensional posteriors (2 free parameters), formal convergence diagnostics (Gelman-Rubin R-hat < 1.01, effective sample size per parameter > 1000) were not reported. The 1-sigma credible regions may have residual Monte Carlo uncertainty. Increasing N_samples or checking chain autocorrelation would quantify this.

source.note: 'Full campaign with N_samples=1e5 completed successfully (~30k chain samples after thinning and burn-in removal). Acceptance rate ~42%.' No explicit R-hat or ESS values reported.

assumptions.description: Priors: log-uniform v [5e-4, 50] GeV, uniform g_D [0.3, 1.5]. The posteriors do not hit prior boundaries, but the prior width relative to the posterior is uneven (factor ~100 for v, factor ~3 for g_D).
assumptions.required_solution: The priors are wide enough to not truncate the posteriors, which is good. The log-uniform prior for v is appropriate for a scale parameter. However, the specific choice of prior bounds (5e-4 to 50 GeV for v, 0.3 to 1.5 for g_D) could affect the Bayesian evidence calculation (if desired) and the shape of the posterior in low-probability regions. A sensitivity check with wider or different priors would confirm robustness.

source.note: Prior ranges stated. 'Posteriors do not hit prior boundaries'.

assumptions.description: Kinetic mixing epsilon is completely unspecified. All 5 directly testable A' constraints (LHCb, BaBar, NA64, NA62, BBN) depend on epsilon values.
assumptions.required_solution: This is the critical blocking issue for the constraint analysis. Without epsilon, one cannot determine whether the 1-sigma preferred region (m_A' = 300-700 MeV) is excluded or allowed. The applicable epsilon range covers at least 10 orders of magnitude (10^{-10} to 10^{-3}). The constraint analysis identifies a viable window 10^{-9} < epsilon < 10^{-4} where A' satisfies all constraints, but this cannot be verified without an epsilon value. Priority: high.

source.note: 'Kinetic mixing epsilon is unspecified. Most dark photon constraints depend on epsilon.'

assumptions.description: Higgs portal coupling lambda_HPhi is completely unspecified. All rho constraints (BBN, CMB, beam dumps, Higgs exotic decays) depend on the scalar mixing angle sin^2(theta) which is proportional to lambda_HPhi.
assumptions.required_solution: Without lambda_HPhi (or equivalently sin^2(theta)), the cosmological viability of the dark scalar rho cannot be evaluated. The combined BBN+CMB exclusion covers sin^2(theta) from ~10^{-11} to ~10^{-6} for m_rho = 50-100 MeV (arXiv:1812.07585). Only sin^2(theta) < 10^{-11} is clearly viable. Whether the preferred parameter region falls in the allowed or excluded range depends entirely on the choice of lambda_HPhi. Priority: high.

source.note: 'Higgs portal lambda_HPhi is unspecified.'

assumptions.description: The BBN constraint on the dark photon gives a LOWER bound on epsilon (epsilon > 10^{-9}-10^{-8} for decay before BBN), not an upper bound. This is often misunderstood.
assumptions.required_solution: Coy et al. (2025, arXiv:2405.10792) explicitly show that for m_A'~300-700 MeV, the requirement that A' decays before BBN (tau' < 0.2 s) implies epsilon > 10^{-9}-10^{-8}. This is a lower bound because weaker mixing means longer lifetimes. Combined with the upper bounds from collider experiments (epsilon < 10^{-4} from LHCb/BaBar), the viable window is 10^{-9} < epsilon < 10^{-4}. This window must be checked against the thermalization requirement for xi=1.

source.note: CONST-004: BBN dark photon decay constraint gives LOWER bound on epsilon.

assumptions.description: If both visible (A' -> SM via epsilon) and invisible (A' -> rho + A'*) decay channels are open, the actual branching ratio determines which specific collider constraints apply.
affected_outputs: Applicability of LHCb, BaBar, NA62 constraints
assumptions.required_solution: The competition between visible and invisible decay channels depends on the relative size of epsilon (determining visible width) and g_D (determining invisible width to rho+A'*). For the preferred region (g_D ~ 0.5-0.55), the invisible width via g_D may dominate if epsilon is small. In that case, the BaBar invisible constraint (epsilon < 3x10^{-4}) is more relevant than the LHCb muon constraint. A full computation of A' branching fractions is needed.

source.note: 'Dark photon constraints assume either visible decay or invisible decay. Actual branching ratio depends on unknown epsilon.'

assumptions.description: Delta N_eff from dark radiation: with xi=1 and dark sector dofs = 4, Delta N_eff ~ 0.57, exceeding Planck 95% CL bound (0.3).
assumptions.required_solution: This is arguably the most serious uncontrolled uncertainty. The xi=1 assumption directly implies Delta N_eff ~ 0.57, which is in tension with Planck. Possible resolutions: (1) The supercooled FOPT dilutes dark sector energy density before BBN/CMB (needs computation); (2) The dark sector is not thermalized with SM (contradicts xi=1); (3) The dark sector particles decay and dump their entropy into SM before BBN (requires specific portal couplings). The literature (arXiv:2602.14324) shows that thermal history treatment can resolve this tension. The current pipeline does not compute post-PT dark sector abundance.

source.note: CONST-007: Delta N_eff from dark radiation. 'This is a serious tension with the xi=1 assumption.'

assumptions.description: Dark scalar (rho) freeze-in yield and relic overproduction constraints cannot be evaluated without lambda_HPhi.
assumptions.required_solution: For sin^2(theta) > 10^{-6}, the scalar thermalizes and freeze-out overproduces the relic density (excluded). For 10^{-12} < sin^2(theta) < 10^{-7}, freeze-in produces a subdominant population that must decay before BBN/CMB. The combined BBN+CMB exclusion from the 'cosmological beam dump' (arXiv:1812.07585) covers nearly the entire natural range. Only sin^2(theta) < 10^{-11} is clearly safe. Computing the actual freeze-in yield for the preferred m_rho range (50-100 MeV) and a given lambda_HPhi would determine viability.

source.note: CONST-023: Dark scalar freeze-in and relic overproduction

assumptions.description: Several rho constraints (PS191 beam dump, LHC exotic Higgs decays) only partially cover the m_rho = 30-70 MeV 1-sigma range, and some require recasting.
assumptions.required_solution: The PS191 constraint covers m_rho ~100-150 MeV, partially overlapping the upper end of the 1-sigma range. CMS EXO-24-034 probes h->SS for m_S > 0.4 GeV, which is above m_rho. NA62/E949 probes K->pi+invisible, which covers m_rho < m_K-m_pi (satisfied). The coverage is incomplete in the lower range (30-50 MeV). For a comprehensive constraint analysis, all applicable channels should be computed.

source.note: CONST-011: 'Partially covers 1-sigma m_rho range'. CONST-021: 'm_rho is below CMS sensitivity threshold'.

assumptions.description: Numerical failures: 204 out of 925 FOPT scan points failed (mostly 'Tn invalid: nan' for small v/g_D).
assumptions.required_solution: 22% of scan points failed numerically. The failures cluster at small v (< 0.02 GeV) and small g_D (< 0.5), exactly where the phase transition would be too weak or the CW mechanism produces numerically unstable potentials. The failures likely represent genuinely non-viable regions, but the boundary of the viable region is determined by numerical stability rather than a physical criterion. A more robust backend or adaptive stepping could map the boundary more cleanly.

source.note: 204 numerical failures, 41 physical failures (eternal inflation).

assumptions.description: Eternal inflation flagged in 41 scan points (g_D > certain threshold depending on v), preventing those points from being considered viable.
assumptions.required_solution: The eternal inflation condition is correctly identified by the backend and represents a genuine physical boundary: if the FOPT never completes because quantum fluctuations dominate over classical evolution, the transition fails. The excluded points (g_D > ~0.65-0.85 depending on v) are correctly discarded. This is a controlled exclusion.

source.note: 41 physical failures due to eternal inflation.

assumptions.description: Athron et al. (2024, arXiv:2306.17239) challenge about false vacuum trapping for supercooled FOPTs is relevant but not directly addressed by this pipeline.
assumptions.required_solution: Athron et al. (2024) showed that for a specific model (non-linearly realized EW symmetry), supercooled FOPTs fail to complete at MeV-scale temperatures. However, conformal models circumvent this because S_E -> 0 as T -> 0 (as noted in arXiv:2501.11619). The backend includes a percolation check via I(T) condition, which passed for viable points. But a dedicated percolation analysis following arXiv:2501.15649 (which checks percolation AND completion) would provide stronger confidence.

source.note: 'False vacuum trapping: Athron et al. (2024) raised concerns... conformal nature may circumvent this but careful percolation checking is still needed'

assumptions.description: GW source modeling: whether sound waves or bubble collisions dominate in the extreme supercooling regime is debated in the literature.
assumptions.required_solution: For alpha >> 1 (vacuum-dominated), the dbf template accounting for bulk flow and dissipation is the most appropriate choice. However, the LISA Cosmology Working Group (Caprini et al. 2024) and several papers (arXiv:2501.15649, arXiv:2602.14324) use different prescriptions. The choice of dbf template is physically motivated but represents a modeling uncertainty. A comparison with alternative templates (e.g., envelope approximation for bubble collisions) would quantify the systematic uncertainty.

source.note: 'GW source modelling: Whether sound waves or bubble collisions dominate in strongly supercooled transitions is debated'

assumptions.description: The PTA benchmark scan used the 'score' metric (number of NANOGrav frequency bins inside 1-sigma violin), which is a simple bin-counting statistic rather than a full likelihood.
assumptions.required_solution: The benchmark scan scores points by how many of the 14 NG15 frequency bins fall within the 1-sigma violin contours. This is a useful heuristic but does not capture correlations between bins or the full spectral shape. The best-fit points (14/14) all match all bins, so the score is saturated. The PTArcade Bayesian analysis (using the full CEFFyL likelihood) provides the statistically rigorous inference. The benchmark scan is a useful coarse filter but should not be over-interpreted.

source.note: Score metric: number of bins inside violins. Best-fit points all score 14/14.

assumptions.description: Model comparison (Bayes factor) between the dark U(1) model and the SMBHB model was not computed.
assumptions.required_solution: The PTArcade run did not compute marginal likelihood / Bayesian evidence. Most PTA FOPT papers (e.g., arXiv:2501.11619, arXiv:2502.19478) report Bayes factors vs the SMBHB model. Without this, the statistical preference of the data for the FOPT model over the standard SMBHB interpretation cannot be quantified. This is a missing figure of merit that is standard in the field.

source.note: 'evidence: null, evidence_error: null'. Evidence not computed.

assumptions.description: The PTArcade campaign was reported as 'partial chains' in the PTA handoff, with the full 1e5-sample run still processing. The final analysis used ~5700 post-burn-in samples.
assumptions.required_solution: The PTA report notes the campaign was still running when the handoff was produced. The partial chains (5700 post-burn-in samples) may not have reached full convergence, especially in the tails of the distribution. A completed run with convergence checks would provide more robust inference. The handoff_pta.json posterior (v ~ [0.62, 1.23] GeV, g_D ~ [0.509, 0.545]) should be considered preliminary until the full chain is analyzed.

source.note: Partial chains used. 'Full campaign with N_samples=1e5 completed successfully' but posterior from partial chains.

assumptions.description: No non-Gaussianity, polarization, or frequency-dependent speed of sound effects were modeled in the GW spectrum.
affected_outputs: GW spectrum shape at high precision
assumptions.required_solution: These effects are subdominant for the current analysis, which focuses on the overall PTA band match. Non-Gaussianity and polarization are undetectable with current PTA sensitivity. Higher-order effects will become relevant for future PTA datasets (e.g., SKA).

source.note: 'No non-Gaussianity, polarisation, or frequency-dependent speed of sound effects were modeled'

assumptions.description: The backend uses interpolated g_star(T) from T_vs_g_gs_GeV.csv. SM particle content is not explicitly tracked.
assumptions.required_solution: The g_star interpolation is the standard approach used in the literature. The table covers the relevant temperature range (MeV-GeV) with sufficient resolution. This is a controlled approximation.

source.note: 'SM particle content not explicitly listed. Handled via interpolated g_star(T) table.'

assumptions.description: The 4 directly-testable theory constraints (perturbativity, CW consistency, classical scale invariance) are all satisfied, providing a baseline of model health.
assumptions.required_solution: No action needed. The theory-level checks confirm the model is internally consistent.

source.note: CONST-008, CONST-009, CONST-016 all passed/satisfied.

assumptions.description: MAP values from PTArcade (v=1.07 GeV, g_D=0.508) and benchmark scan BP1 (v=0.43 GeV, g_D=0.533) differ significantly, especially for v.
assumptions.required_solution: The PTArcade MAP (v=1.07 GeV) is a factor ~2.5 larger than the benchmark scan BP1 (v=0.43 GeV). This discrepancy arises because the benchmark scan uses a simple bin-counting score, while PTArcade uses the full CEFFyL likelihood. The likelihood method is more reliable. The 1-sigma ranges from PTArcade (v: 0.62-1.23 GeV) capture this uncertainty.

source.note: BP1: v=0.43. MAP: v=1.07. Different by factor ~2.5.

assumptions.description: No backend module exists for computing A' decay widths, rho decay widths, freeze-in yields, or dark sector thermal history. These are all identified as 'missing calculations' by the constraint agent.
affected_outputs: All experimental constraints (19/23 partially or fully blocked)
assumptions.required_solution: The constraint agent identified 8 specific missing calculations (see handoff_constraints.json section 'missing_calculations') with high priority. These require new backend modules for: (1) A' decay width and branching fractions, (2) rho decay width and branching fractions, (3) freeze-in yield for rho, (4) dark sector temperature evolution xi(T), (5) post-PT residual abundance. Without these, no definitive constraint can be applied. This is the single most impactful limitation of the current pipeline.

source.note: 8 missing calculations listed, 7 with high priority.

required_followups.description: Implement A' decay width and branching fraction computation (SM visible channels via epsilon, dark sector invisible channel via rho+A'*). Needed to apply LHCb, BaBar, NA62, NA64, and BBN constraints.
required_followups.suggested_action: Create backend module for computing A' partial widths. For visible decays: use R-ratio interpolation for hadronic channels up to m_A' ~ 700 MeV, plus leptonic channels. For invisible: compute A' -> rho + A'* (off-shell) width via dark gauge coupling. Compare branching fractions to determine which collider constraints apply.

required_followups.description: Specify benchmark or prior values for kinetic mixing epsilon and Higgs portal lambda_HPhi. This unblocks 19 of 23 constraints.
required_followups.suggested_action: Either (a) treat epsilon and lambda_HPhi as free parameters and scan them, or (b) fix them to benchmark values from the literature (e.g., epsilon ~ 10^{-10} for thermalization, going up to epsilon ~ 10^{-5} for visible coupling; lambda_HPhi chosen such that sin^2(theta) < 10^{-11} to avoid BBN/CMB constraints), or (c) treat them as nuisance parameters with broad priors and marginalize.

required_followups.description: Implement synchronous dark sector thermal history computation (xi(T) evolution) to resolve the Delta N_eff tension.
required_followups.suggested_action: Follow the methodology of Li & Nath (2026, arXiv:2602.14324) to compute synchronous thermal evolution of xi = T_dark/T_SM. This involves solving Boltzmann equations for energy density exchange between dark and visible sectors. With xi=1, Delta N_eff ~ 0.57 > 0.3 (Planck bound). A proper thermal history may show xi decreases before BBN, alleviating the tension.

required_followups.description: Compute rho freeze-in yield and decay lifetime to evaluate BBN/CMB constraints from the cosmological beam dump.
required_followups.suggested_action: Implement freeze-in production calculation for rho via Higgs mixing (following Fradette et al. 2018, arXiv:1812.07585). For m_rho = 30-70 MeV and sin^2(theta) < 10^{-11}, the relic is negligible. For larger mixing, the combined BBN+CMB exclusion must be checked.

required_followups.description: Compute mean bubble separation R* directly from the nucleation history rather than using beta/H, to cross-check the validity of the beta/H parameterization for extreme supercooling.
required_followups.suggested_action: Implement R* = n_b^{-1/3} computation by integrating the nucleation rate over the full transition history. Compare with beta/H-derived values. This would validate the current results or quantify the systematic uncertainty.

required_followups.description: Complete the PTArcade Bayesian analysis with formal convergence diagnostics (R-hat, ESS) and report Bayes factors vs SMBHB model.
required_followups.suggested_action: Re-run the PTArcade campaign with N_samples = 2-5e5 to ensure convergence. Report R-hat < 1.01 for all parameters, ESS > 1000. Compute log Bayes factor vs SMBHB model using the Savage-Dickey density ratio or nested sampling.

required_followups.description: Quantify the RG scheme and scale dependence of FOPT parameters by comparing the fixed-scale backend results with an RG-improved calculation.
required_followups.suggested_action: Implement the scale-setting prescription from arXiv:2501.11619 (mu = max[M_Z'(phi_s), pi T]) or compare against the 3D EFT results from arXiv:2511.02910. This would provide a systematic uncertainty estimate on alpha and beta/H.

required_followups.description: Check the runaway bubble wall assumption by computing NLO friction (Bodeker-Moore pressure) for the preferred parameter points.
required_followups.suggested_action: Implement the Bodeker-Moore friction calculation as in arXiv:2501.15649. For supercooled transitions with alpha ~ 10^11, the vacuum pressure likely dominates over friction, so walls are probably runaway. But a quantitative check would confirm this.

required_followups.description: Extend the benchmark scan to cover the full FOPT viable region with finer resolution around the PTA-preferred Posterior boundary.
blocked_by: None, but lower priority after PTArcade analysis
required_followups.suggested_action: The current scan (925 FOPT + 1600 PTA benchmark points) provides reasonable coverage. Optional: refine the region v = 0.5-2 GeV, g_D = 0.49-0.55 with higher resolution to better resolve the PTA-preferred region boundaries.

required_followups.description: Perform template sensitivity study by comparing dbf results with alternative GW templates for extreme supercooling.
blocked_by: Requires implementing multiple templates in PTArcade model
required_followups.suggested_action: Implement and compare at least two templates (e.g., dbf and envelope/bubble collision) for a subset of benchmark points. Assess the resulting shift in GW amplitude and peak frequency. If the difference is small (< 20%), the template choice is not a dominant uncertainty.
